coredata.dc:title: Nucleus-targeted N-doped carbon dots via DNA-binding for imaging of hypochlorous in cells and zebrafish
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Hao
Pang, Lan-Fang
Wei, Na
Guo, Xiao-Feng
Wang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: We reported N-doped CDs (MTCDs) with excellent nucleus-targeted ability for selectively detecting ClO−. MTCDs can be easily prepared using m-phenylenediamine and 1,2,3-propanetricarboxylic acid as precursors through a solvothermal method. The maximum excitation/emission wavelengths were located at 444/513 nm, with QYs calculated to be 11.4 %. The fluorescence of MTCDs could be selectively quenched by ClO- upon the other ions, reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS). The quenching coefficient showed a good linearity versus CClO- in the range of 1–100 μM, with detection limit of 0.48 μM. MTCDs were found to have splendid nuclear localization capabilities in MCF-7, Hela and H1299 cells and were successfully applied to image ClO− in cell nucleus and zebrafishes. During the nuclear localization mechanism study, we found a very interesting phenomenon: MTCDs were rare negatively charged CDs targeting nucleus by binding to the groove of DNA through hydrogen bond, which were different with most reported positively charged nucleus-targeted CDs via electrostatic adsorption. This finding proved that negatively charged CDs could also win the nucleus-targeted ability. Besides, this work provided a new strategy for designing nucleus-targeted CDs as well as the research of targeted release of drugs and cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nucleus-targeted
Carbon dots
Hypochlorous
ClO−
DNA oxidative damage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preliminary experiments and discover of unexpected nucleus-targeting ability
Fluorescent imaging of nucleus in living cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of MTCDs. (B) Size distribution of MTCDs obtained by counting the diameters of 94 carbon dots in TEM images.
FT-IR spectra of tricarballylate, m-PDA and MTCDs.
(A) XPS spectra of MTCDs. (B) Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s. (C) Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s.
Solid lines: UV–vis absorption of tricarballylate, m-PDA, and MTCDs. Dotted lines: Normalized excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of MTCDs.

Selectivity investigation of MTCDs to ClO−. F: Fluorescence intensity of MTCDs with 100 μM of Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cd2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ag+, Fe2+, NO2 −, NO3 −, ·OH, O2 −, ONOO−, 1O2, H2O2, NO and ClO−; F0: Initial fluorescence intensity of MTCDs without adding any substances above.

(A) The quenching efficiency of MTCDs versus ClO− concentrations. F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of MTCDs in the absence and presence of ClO−, respectively. (B) The corresponding fluorescence spectra of MTCDs after added different concentrations of ClO−.

Fluorescence intensity decay curves of MTCDs with (red) and without (black) 50 μM of NaClO. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
MTT reduction rates in MCF-7 cells upon different concentration of MTCDs from 0 to 500 μg mL−1 for 24 h.

CLSM images of Hela cells, MCF-7 cells and H1299 cells stained with Hoechst 33342 and MTCDs. The above cells were incubated with MTCDs (200 μg mL−1) for 4 h and Hoechst 33342 for another 15 min at 37 °C. Oil immersion lens: 60 × . Excitation channel wavelength: 405 nm. Emission channel wavelengths: 447 nm for Hoechst 33342 and 525 nm for MTCDs.

CLSM images of MCF-7 cells incubated with 200 μg mL−1 of MTCDs for 4 h and then incubated with 0 μM, 25 μM, 50 μM and 100 μM of ClO− for 15 min at 37 °C. Oil immersion lens: 60 × . Excitation channel wavelength: 405 nm. Emission channel wavelengths: 525 nm.

Fluorescence microscopic images of ClO− in zebrafishes. (A) Without MTCDs. (B) Incubating with 200 μg∙mL-1 of MTCDs at 28 °C for 6 h. (C) Incubating with 200 μg∙mL-1 of MTCDs at 28 °C for 6 h, and 750 μM of ClO− at 28 °C for another 10 min. Fluorescence microscopic images were taken through a green optical filter. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

MCF-7 cells incubated with 200 μg⋅mL−1 of MTCDs for 4 h and then fixed with −20 °C methanol. Fixed cells were digested with 25 μg⋅mL−1 of DNase or RNase for 2 h. Oil immersion lens: 40 × . Excitation channel wavelength: 405 nm. Emission channel wavelengths: 525 nm.
(A): CD spectra of DNA mixed with MTCDs at different ratios. (B): UV–vis net absorption spectra of DNA with MTCDs at different ratios.

head.ce:title: Nucleus-targeted N-doped carbon dots via DNA-binding for imaging of hypochlorous in cells and zebrafish

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We reported N-doped CDs (MTCDs) with excellent nucleus-targeted ability for selectively detecting ClO−. MTCDs can be easily prepared using m-phenylenediamine and 1,2,3-propanetricarboxylic acid as precursors through a solvothermal method. The maximum excitation/emission wavelengths were located at 444/513 nm, with QYs calculated to be 11.4 %. The fluorescence of MTCDs could be selectively quenched by ClO- upon the other ions, reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS). The quenching coefficient showed a good linearity versus CClO- in the range of 1–100 μM, with detection limit of 0.48 μM. MTCDs were found to have splendid nuclear localization capabilities in MCF-7, Hela and H1299 cells and were successfully applied to image ClO− in cell nucleus and zebrafishes. During the nuclear localization mechanism study, we found a very interesting phenomenon: MTCDs were rare negatively charged CDs targeting nucleus by binding to the groove of DNA through hydrogen bond, which were different with most reported positively charged nucleus-targeted CDs via electrostatic adsorption. This finding proved that negatively charged CDs could also win the nucleus-targeted ability. Besides, this work provided a new strategy for designing nucleus-targeted CDs as well as the research of targeted release of drugs and cancer treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: As one species of typical ROS, ClO− is involved in intracellular signaling pathways, redox balance [1,2] and regulation of the physiological functions of cells [3]. ClO− with a high level is related to diseases such as tissue damage, hepatic ischemia-reperfusion injury, lung injury, rheumatoid, cardiovascular disease, neuronal degeneration and joint inflammation [4]. The exposure of DNA nucleobases to ClO− leads to the formation of different stable chlorinated products [5]. Such case will result in base and nucleotide modifications thus cause DNA damage [6], and will finally cause some macroscopic or microscopic diseases, even some cancers [1,7,8]. Therefore, detection of ClO− in cells and organisms is of great significance for revealing the physiological and pathological functions of ClO− in cells and organisms metabolism.

Carbon dots (CDs) possess unique traits including high brightness, resistance to photo bleaching, excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility and cell permeability [9,10]. Due to the mentioned merits, CDs have been gradually prepared as fluorescence probe in ClO− detection [11–28]. Among them, most CDs were used for the quantitative analysis of ClO− in water and biological samples because their emission wavelengths were often near 450 nm or less with maximum excitation wavelength under 400 nm [11–17]. For ClO− bioimaging applications, CDs with long-wavelength emission are preferred, which can efficiently reduce phototoxicity and biological autofluorescence interference. By attaching fluorescein fluorephore to the surface of CDs, fluorescent emission larger than 500 nm was obtained after modification [13,18]. Meanwhile, doping with heteroatom is another way to get fascinating long-wavelength emission for CDs. Acid fuchsin, rose bengal and safranine T have been used as precursors to prepare heteroatom-doped CDs which fluoresce at 552 nm [19], 525 nm [20] and 590 nm [21], respectively. Though some good work had been done, there still exist challenges that restrict the wide application of CDs in ClO− imaging. Firstly, some ions—especially heavy metal ions—can easily affect the fluorescence of CDs [18,20,22–25]. Secondly, despite the complicated post-modification methods, unmodified CDs with long emission wavelength for ClO− biosensing and bioimaging are still less, which is still worthy of further research [29]. What’s more, there are few organelles-guided CDs reported for ClO− imaging.

In this study, we tried to improve the performance of CDs by doping with aromatic nitrogen [12,30]. So N-doped CDs (MTCDs) with green fluorescence emission for detection of ClO− were prepared by a simple solvothermal method using m-phenylenediamine and 1,2,3-propanetricarboxylic acid as the precursors. Results showed that MTCDs had splendid selectivity and sensitivity toward ClO−. But surprisingly, MTCDs were found to have excellent nucleus-targeted capacity in MCF-7 cells, Hela cells and H1299 cells without any modification process. Even weirder was that MTCDs had a negatively zeta potential of -12.7 eV, which was completely different with widely used positively charged nucleus-targeted CDs. We put forward a possible targeting mechanism after further research. To our best knowledge, MTCDs were the first negatively charged nucleus-targeted CDs, and could be applied for imaging of ClO− in cell nucleus.

Absolute ethanol, DMSO, KBr (Spectrumpure), NaOH, Na3PO4, H3PO4, KCl, NaCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, AlCl3, CuCl2·2H2O, (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, SnCl2·2H2O, ZnCl2, AgNO3, BaCl2·2H2O, Pb(COO)2·3H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, MnCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, H2O2, NaClO, NaNO2, NaNO3, KO2, KMnO4, Hoechst 33342 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). m-phenylenediamine(m-PDA) and 1,2,3-propanetricarboxylic acid were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Genview. Hydroxyl radical (·OH) was generated in the Fenton system from (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O and H2O2, The final concentration was standardized by titration with potassium permanganate [31]. 1O2 was generated from H2O2/Na2MoO4 reaction (Two H2O2 molecules formed one 1O2 molecule) [32]. NOC13 was prepared following literature method and using as a nitric oxide (NO) donor [33]. ONOO− was synthesized from NaNO2 and H2O2, and the concentration was calculated by measuring the absorbance at 302 nm (ε = 1670 M-1 cm-1). KO2 was dissolved in DMSO and diluted to the required concentration with phosphate buffered solution (pH 7.4) during use. 30 % H2O2 solutions were diluted with phosphate buffered solution (pH 7.4) for further use.

Hela cells, MCF-7 cells and H1299 cells were purchased from Center for Animal Experiment (Wuhan University, Wuhan, China). Unless otherwise stated, all the reagents mentioned above were of analytical grade, and were used without further purification. All the water used was deionized water purified by the Milli-Q system (Resistivity:18.25 MΩ).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan) with working voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed on an iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc, America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi XPS System (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc, America). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained from a UV-2500 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu Japan). Fluorescence spectra were collected with a RF-6000 Fluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Unless otherwise stated, the excitation and emission slit widths were both set as 3 nm, with excitation/emission wavelengths set as 444/513 nm. The pH values of test solutions were measured with a FE20 pH meter (Mettler-Toledo, China). Cell imaging was performed by Nikon C1 SI confocal laser scanning microscope (Nikon, Japan). MTT assay results were obtained from Thermo MK3 multi-functional microplate reader (Thermo, China). Circular dichroism (CD) signals were collected from Jasco-810 spectropolarimeter (Jasco, USA). Fluorescence microscopic images of ClO− in zebrafishes were obtained through stereo microscope SZX10-3121 equipped with a cooled charge-coupled device (CCD) camera (Olympus DP72) by CellSens Entry image capture software (Olympus).

MTCDs with splendid nucleus-targeted ability can be simply prepared by a solvothermal method. In brief, 420 mg of m-PDA and 500 mg of 1,2,3-propanetricarboxylic acid (tricarballylate) were first dissolved and sonicated in 30 mL of absolute ethanol, then the solution was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction was completed and cooled down to room temperature, a brownish yellow solution was obtained. The solvent was removed by distillation and residue was redissolved in deionized water. The above solution was dialyzed against deionized water with a cellulose dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) for 24 h, then dialysate was centrifuged at 5000 rpm to remove large particulate matter. The obtained MTCDs solution was lyophilized with a freeze dryer, then collected and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. For experimental use, 5 mg of MTCDs were first dissolved in 1 mL of deionized water to prepare a stock solution, and then diluted to the desired concentration.

The QYs of MTCDs were measured using quinine sulfate (Φ R = 0.58) as the reference [34]. The QYs of MTCDs (Φ S) were calculated using the formula below:
(1)
Φ S = (Φ RODRISηS 2)/(ODSIRηR 2)

R and S referred to quinine sulfate and MTCDs, respectively. Φ referred to the QYs of the testing samples, OD was the optical density measured at 359 nm with a value between 0.01 and 0.1, I referred to the integrated emission fluorescence intensity collected with excitation wavelength at 359 nm, η was the refractive index. MTCDs were dissolved in deionized water. Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution.

Influence of pH on MTCDs’ fluorescence behavior was further investigated. Briefly, 3 mL phosphate buffer solutions with various pH ranging from 3 to 12 were first added to different centrifuge tubes, then 20 μL of MTCDs (5 mg∙mL−1) were added and kept at 37 °C for 20 min in a water bath. The fluorescence emission intensities at 513 nm were recorded immediately by RF-6000 fluorophotometer.

For the fluorescent detection of ClO−, 20 μL of MTCDs (5 mg∙mL-1) and different amounts of ClO− were transferred into a 5 mL centrifuge tube, then diluted to 3 mL with phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 7.4). The above solutions were kept at 37 °C for 20 min and the fluorescence intensities at 513 nm were immediately recorded. Excitation-dependence behaviors of MTCDs were obtained with excitation wavelengths changing from 394 nm to 484 nm.

In order to better estimate the application performance of MTCDs for ClO−, Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cd2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ag+, Fe2+, NO2 −, NO3 −,
OH, O2 −, ONOO−, 1O2, H2O2 and NO were chosen as co-existed substances to check the selectivity of MTCDs to ClO−. All the above substances were dissolved in phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 7.4) with concentrations of 100 μM. After incubated with MTCDs for 20 min in 37 °C water bath, the fluorescence intensities at 513 nm were recorded with RF-6000 Fluorophotometer.

Hela cells, MCF-7 cells and H1299 cells were cultured in high-glucose Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1 % penicillin, and 1 % streptomycin in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.

Before imaging, the cytotoxicity of MTCDs to cells was examined. Briefly, MCF-7 cells were cultured in a 96-well plate with a density of 1 × 10−4 cells per well for 24 h. Then the mediums were replaced with DMEM containing different concentrations of MTCDs from 20 to 500 μg∙mL-1. After incubated for another 24 h, MTT assay was performed to estimate the cytotoxicity of MTCDs to MCF-7 cells.

For imaging studies, cells were first incubated with MTCDs (200 μg·mL−1) for 4 h, then the mediums were replaced to remove the redundant MTCDs. After that, different concentrations of NaClO (diluted with HyClone PBS buffer) were added to investigate the detection ability of MTCDs to exogenous ClO−in MCF-7 cells. PBS buffer was used to wash the cells three times immediately before imaging. Cell imaging was performed on Nikon C1 SI confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) with a 60 × oil immersion lens. The excitation and emission channels were set as 405 nm and 525 nm, respectively.

To validate the localization ability of MTCDs to cell nucleus, MCF-7 cells, Hela cells and H1299 cells were incubated with 200 μg∙mL−1 of MTCDs at 37 °C for 4 h and 10 μg∙mL−1 of a commercial nuclear probe Hoechst 33342 for another 15 min. Then the cells were washed and observed under confocal laser scanning microscope. The excitation channel was set as 405 nm, the emission channels were set as 447 nm for Hoechst 33342 and 525 nm for MTCDs, respectively.

For in-vivo imaging, zebrafish were incubated with 200 μg∙mL−1 of MTCDs at 28 °C for 6 h, and then incubated with 750 μM of NaClO at 28 °C for another 10 min. The medium was replaced with deionized water and observed under SZX10-3121 stereo microscope with a green filter.

MCF-7 cells were first incubated with 200 μg∙mL−1 of MTCDs at 37 °C for 4 h, then washed with PBS for three times. The above cells were fixed by 1 mL precooled methanol (−20 °C) for 15 min at a −20 °C refrigerator, and washed with PBS for another three times. DNase Ⅰ (RNase free, 25 μg∙mL−1 in cell media) and RNase (DNase free, 25μg∙mL−1 in cell media) were added to the above cells and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. The above cells were washed with PBS and used for CLSM imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary experiments and discover of unexpected nucleus-targeting ability
ce:section.ce:para: 1,2,3-propanetricarboxylic acid and m-PDA were chosen as precursors to form the skeleton of CDs and make the surface of CDs rich in nitrogen-containing groups. After MTCDs were designed and successfully prepared, some preliminary experiments were performed. MTCDs were found to emit bright green fluorescence at 513 nm under the maximum excitation wavelength of 444 nm. This bright fluorescence could be selectively quenched by ClO− with a good sensitivity compared with other common ions, ROS and RNS. After that, preliminary cell imaging experiments were performed. From CLSM pictures, strong fluorescence signal was observed almost only in nucleus, which indicated that MTCDs may have nucleus-targeting ability. At the beginning, we suspected that MTCDs may have positive potential, and could interact with the negatively charged components of chromosome by electrostatic force—just like most other reported nucleus-staining CDs. But to our surprise, the zeta potential of MTCDs was tested to be -12.7 eV, which indicated this phenomenon was unlikely caused by electrostatic attraction. So the properties and possible nuclear localization mechanism of MTCDs became the key points of this work.

The morphology of MTCDs was characterized by TEM. Fig. 1 showed that MTCDs were uniform in size, monodispersed and approximately circular. The particle size of MTCDs were distributed at 4.34 ± 1.13 nm. FT-IR and XPS were carried out to study the functional groups and chemical bonds on the surface of MTCDs (Fig. 2 ). FT-IR spectra comparison of MTCDs and the starting materials showed that the absorption peaks at 3395.21 cm−1, 3326.50 cm−1 and 1624.32 cm−1 in m-PDA were almost disappeared in MTCDs, which attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibration, symmetric stretching vibration and bending vibration peaks of -NH2 groups, respectively. Absorption peaks at 3058.16 cm−1,1454.98 cm−1 and 745.65 cm−1 in MTCDs attributed to the unsaturated ring of the aromatic ring CH stretching vibration, the skeleton stretching vibration of CC bond, and the fingerprint peak of the aromatic ring. It should be pointed out that a significant absorption peak at 1732.32 cm-1 were appeared in MTCDs’ FT-IR spectrum, which attributed to the characteristic peaks of C=O stretching vibrations in NHCOO bond. FT-IR spectra comparison above indicated that the starting materials were well converted to the skeleton of MTCDs. XPS survey showed that MTCDs consisted of 79.51 % of C, 12.62 % of N and 7.87 % of O, in addition to the hydrogen atoms. Besides, four peaks were deconvoluted from C1s spectrum (Fig. 3 B), corresponding to CC/CC bond at 284.8 eV, CO bond at 285.9 eV, CO bond at 288.0 eV and CN bond at 285.3 eV [35,36]. In addition, deconvoluted N1s spectrum at 398.9 eV, 3995 eV, and 400.2 eV revealed the existence of pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N in the skeleton of MTCDs [37,38]. XPS survey results indicated that the surface of MTCDs were rich in functional groups such as carboxyl groups and nitrogen-containing groups, which were well consistent with the results of FT-IR spectra.

Fig. 4 showed the UV–vis absorption spectra of MTCDs and the starting materials. In addition to the strong absorption peak at 285 nm for m-PDA, a new broad absorption peak appeared in the range of 310−500 nm for MTCDs. The newly emerging absorption peak was well coincident with the excitation peak of MTCDs, which also indicated the successfully preparation of MTCDs by precursors. The maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of MTCDs were located at 444/513 nm, with QYs calculated to be 11.4 % (The detailed calculation process were listed in Fig. S1 and Table S1). Fig. S2 showed the emission wavelengths of MTCDs when excitation wavelengths changed from 394 nm to 484 nm. We could find that the emission wavelengths slowly shifted to the red region when the excitation wavelengths gradually increased. This phenomenon indicated that MTCDs exhibited the inherent excitation-dependence behavior of CDs [39,40].

When MTCDs were applied for imaging in cells and living organisms, water-solubility, biocompatibility, pH stability and selectivity to ClO− in complex environments were always need to be considered. XPS and the FT-IR analysis proved that large amounts of hydrophilic groups such as amino and carboxyl groups were on the surface of MTCDs, which made MTCDs very water soluble. Fig. S3 showed the pH sensitivity investigation of MTCDs in phosphate buffer solutions. We could find that the fluorescence intensity of MTCDs kept stable over a broad pH range from 4 to 9, but became attenuated when pH was less than 4 or greater than 9. Fig. 5 showed the selectivity investigation of MTCDs to ClO −compared with other common biologically substances. From the results, 100 μM of Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cd2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ag+, Fe2+, NO2 −, NO3 −, ·OH, O2 −, ONOO−, 1O2, H2O2 and NO had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of MTCDs. However, 100 μM of ClO− almost completely quenched the fluorescence of MTCDs. The pH sensitivity and selectivity investigations conducted above indicated that MTCDs had the potential for detecting ClO− in complex cellular and organismal environments.

Before the detection of ClO− in real samples, the quenching coefficient ((F0-F) / F0) versus different concentrations of ClO− (1, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 μM) were studied first. Fig. 6 showed that the fluorescence intensities of the MTCDs at 513 nm were gradually decreased with the increase of ClO−concentrations (CClO −). The quenching coefficient showed a good linearity versus CClO − in the range of 1–100 μM. The correlation equations were (F0-F)/F0 =0.023 + 0.009 CClO −, with regression coefficient (R2, n = 3) 0.991. The calculated LOD was as low as 0.48 μM (S/N = 3). To verify the validity of this method, ClO− in tap water was analyzed and reliability was tested by standard addition technique. The ClO− concentration in tap water was found to be 1.36 μM, with recovery rate ranged from 93.5 % to 99.0 % (see Table S2), which indicated that the MTCDs had an excellent sensitivity to ClO−. The comparison of MTCDs with other previously reported CDs for ClO− detection were listed in Table S3. We can see that MTCDs not only had wide linear range, low LOD and long wavelength, but also had super nucleus-targeted ability, which had potential in imaging of ClO− in nucleus and in-vivo.

In order to investigate the quenching mechanism of ClO− to MTCDs, fluorescence intensity decay curves of MTCDs were collected and shown in Fig. 7 . The calculated fluorescence lifetime of MTCDs was 5.27 ns, and only got a slight change to 5.05 ns after reacted with 50 μM of ClO−. This phenomenon indicated that ClO− quenched MTCDs by a static quenching process [41]. The most likely quenching mechanism can be described as this: ClO− affected the functional groups on the surface of MTCDs by a coordination or oxidation process, which weakened the surface passivation of MTCDs and caused fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent imaging of nucleus in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: MTT assay was performed first before imaging studies. Fig. 8 showed the MTT reduction rates in MCF-7 cells upon different concentration of MTCDs ranged from 0 to 500 μg∙mL−1. A control group was set as 100 % which only added the corresponding volume of PBS. When the concentrations of MTCDs increased from 20 to 300 μg∙mL−1, the reduction rates of MTT kept above 90 %. Even if the concentration of MTCDs reached as high as 500 μg∙mL−1, the reduction rate of MTT was still higher than 83.5 %. This result indicated that MTCDs had negligible cytotoxicity.

Staining of nucleus is crucial to reveal nuclear morphology and its roles in cell metabolism, growth, differentiation and apoptosis [42]. MTCDs were first applied for fluorescence imaging in Hela cells, MCF-7 cells and H1299 cells to investigate their cellular uptake and nucleus-staining universality. Fig. 9 showed the CLSM images of Hela cells, MCF-7 cells and H1299 cells. We found that all of these cells showed very strong fluorescence after incubated with MTCDs for 4 h, and the fluorescence most concentrated in nucleus. This phenomenon indicated that MTCDs had successfully penetrated cytomembrane and entered into nucleus, which showed an outstanding nucleus-targeted ability. In order to further verify the nuclear localization efficiency of MTCDs, a commercial nuclear localization reagent Hoechst 33342 was applied for fluorescence co-localization imaging. Fig. 9 demonstrated that the stained cells emitted blue fluorescence with Hoechst 33342 and green fluorescence with MTCDs after excited at 405 nm. It needs to be pointed out that the merged images showed an outstanding co-localization coincidence in nucleus, and the co-localization coincidence rates analyzed by the software Image J reached as high as 98.0 %, 97.4 %, and 95.2 % (Fig. S4), respectively. Such results indicated that MTCDs had a really remarkable nucleus-targeted ability in different cells. These excellent properties also indicated that MTCDs had great potential as commercial nuclear localization reagents with good biocompatibility, low cost, easy preparation and longer wavelength.

Fig. 10 showed CLSM images of MCF-7 cells incubated with 200 μg∙mL−1 of MTCDs for 4 h and different amounts of ClO− for 15 min. It could be seen from the figure that the nucleus showed a very strong fluorescence signal after incubated with MTCDs. Then the fluorescence intensity of nucleus began to decrease when 25 and 50 μM of ClO− was added, and finally almost completely quenched by 100 μM of ClO−. The above results indicated that ClO− could not only penetrate cytomembrane, but also could enter into nucleus and quench the fluorescence of MTCDs. This phenomenon may provide a reference for studying the activity of ClO− in nucleus, especially the oxidative damage to DNA [43–45].

Nucleus imaging of ClO− indicated that MTCDs possessed good biocompatibility, outstanding nucleus-targeting ability and excellent response to ClO−. For further investigating their potential chronic toxicity, MTCDs’ uptake in vivo must be evaluated. Zebrafish was chosen for in-vivo imaging because it had proven to be an extremely valuable vertebrate model in numerous biological studies [46]. As shown in Fig. 11 , zebrafishes without MTCDs showed almost none fluorescence emission through a green optical filter at first. After incubating with 200∙μg mL-1 of MTCDs, the zebrafishes were illuminated with bright green fluorescence (Fig. 11B). While the bright green fluorescence were sharply diminished after incubated with 750 μM of ClO− (Fig. 11C). The above experiments proved that MTCDs were not only less in-vivo toxic, but also very biocompatible, which may become a promising tool in ClO− bioimaging and related biomedical applications.

According to the literature, the size of common nuclear pores is about 10 nm [47]. MTCDs had smaller size of 4.34 ± 1.13 nm, and could enter into nucleus from the nuclear pores. To study the possible binding processes of MTCDs to nucleo acids in nucleus, DNase and RNase digestion experiments were conducted. We can see in Fig. 12 that bright green fluorescence were observed in the nucleus of live cells and the control group of fixed cells. When DNase or RNase were added, only weak fluorescence were observed in the nucleoli of DNase-digested cells. While the nucleus still showed bright fluorescence in RNase-digested group, but the nucleoli were hard to observe. This phenomenon indicated that MTCDs mainly bind to DNA in nucleus. However, the mechanism of MTCDs’ nucleus-targeted ability needed to be further investigated.

In general, most reported nucleus-targeted staining reagents worked by electrostatic interaction, groove binding or intercalation binding with DNA. These modes of interaction could be observed by changes in the circular dichroism spectra or the UV spectra of DNA [48]. For example, electrostatic interaction was often very weak, which had little influence on the circular dichroism spectra and the UV spectra of DNA. Hyperchromic effect occurred in the UV spectra of DNA during a groove binding mode because the bases of the DNA were exposed after interacting with the reagents, thus increasing the UV absorption near 260 nm. Conversely, intercalation binding mode would make the DNA duplex more stable, which would cause hypochromism effect. Besides, groove binding mode would influence the circular dichroism spectra of DNA at 250 nm, which was generated by the stacking of DNA bases. In contrast, the intercalation effect caused a more pronounced change both at 250 nm and 280 nm [49]. To our knowledge, most reported CDs for nucleus imaging had highly positive zeta potential, which would attach to the negatively charged components of the chromosome or DNA by electrostatic interactions [50,51].

To study the nucleus-targeted mechanism of MTCDs, the zeta potential was tested to be -12.7 eV, which indicated that the reason of nucleus-targeted ability was unlikely caused by electrostatic attraction. According to the literature [52,53], the widely used nucleus staining reagents Hoechst 33342 targeted nucleus by binding to adenine-thymine-rich regions of DNA in the minor groove. The high co-localization of MTCDs with Hoechst 33342 in nucleus could be observed from Fig. 9, and we speculated that the co-localization of MTCDs may due to a similar process. In order to verify our conjecture, circular dichroism spectra of DNA were collected. Fig. 13 A showed the circular dichroism spectra of DNA mixed with different ratios of MTCDs. We could find that the circular dichroism spectra of DNA changed when the ratios of the MTCDs increased. Compared to its positive peak around 280 nm, the negative peak around 250 nm showed a more obvious change. Since the peak at 250 nm was generated by the stacking of DNA bases [54], MTCDs may bind to DNA bases in the groove of double helix structure. In order to further verify our conclusions, the UV–vis absorption spectra of DNA before and after reacted with MTCDs were also collected. It could be seen from Fig. 13B that the UV–vis absorption spectra of DNA showed a noticeable hyperchromic effect as the concentrations of MTCDs increased. The results of UV–vis absorption experiments were consistent with the CD spectra, and all the above results pointed to the same conclusion: MTCDs bound to the bases of DNA in the groove of double helix structure, then aggregated in the nucleus. It is important to point out that ClO− could quenched the fluorescence of MTCDs in the groove of DNA, which demonstrated that ClO− could reached the groove of DNA. These results could provide an evidence for the investigation of DNA oxidative damage with ClO−.

In summary, N-doped nucleus-targeted carbon dots (MTCDs) with negative charge were prepared with a one-step solvothermal treatment using m-phenylenediamine and 1,2,3-propanetricarboxylic acid as the precursors in absolute ethanol. MTCDs had good water solubility, excellent optical and pH stability, and exhibited high sensitivity and splendid selectivity toward ClO−. Besides, MTCDs had a good nucleus-targeting capacity. Cell co-localization experiments in MCF-7, Hela and H1299 cells showed MTCDs had quite high co-location coincidence with commercial reagent Hoechst 33342. Then, MTCDs were applied to imaging ClO− in cell nucleus and zebrafishes. Furthermore, the nucleus-targeting mechanism of MTCDs was studied and explained. Unlike most reported positive-charged CDs, negative-charged MTCDs obtained nucleus-targeted ability by a DNA groove-binding mode. This work could provide a reference for DNA oxidative damage investigation. We also hope that nucleus-targeted MTCDs could become a potential tool for targeted release of drugs and cancer treatment research.

Hao Wu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Software. Lan-Fang Pang: Software. Na Wei: Software. Xiao-Feng Guo: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Hong Wang: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, Beijing, China (Nos. 21777126 and 32070398). The authors thank the Large-Scale Instrument and Equipment Sharing Foundation of Wuhan University for the support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129626.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hao Wu received his bachelor’s degree in College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University. Now he studies as a doctoral student in the College of Chemistry and Molecular Science of Wuhan University. His research interest is focused on synthesis and applications of fluorescent probe and carbon dots.
Lan-Fang Pang is a doctoral student of College of Chemistry and Molecular Science, Wuhan University.
Na Wei is a doctoral student of College of Chemistry and Molecular Science, Wuhan University.

Xiao-Feng Guo had a bachelor’s degree in chemistry (2004) from Wuhan University in Wuhan, China. In 2004 he joined the Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine in Wuhan University where he obtained his Ph.D. in analytical chemistry in 2009. His main research interests are focused on computational chemistry and small-molecule fluorescent probe.

Hong Wang studied chemistry at Wuhan University and completed her bachelor studies in the college of chemistry and molecular science in 1988. She obtained her Ph.D. at Wuhan University in Wuhan, China, in 1994. She has been working for the molecular probe group in the College of Chemistry and Molecular Science in Wuhan University since 1996. Now, she is the leader of molecular probe group.
